--- SOURCE 1: The Importance of GIS in Emergency Management ---
URL: https://nyuelj.org/2016/11/1439/

SUMMARY:
Starting with FEMA, and moving towards states and local governments, GIS investment should be prioritized for use in disaster management.

FULL CONTENT:
New York University School of Law

![](https://nyuelj.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/world-map-1236956.jpg)

# The Importance of GIS in Emergency Management

By Monika Holser, UCLA School of Law, Class of 2018

This post is part of the [Environmental Law Review Syndicate](http://www.nyuelj.org/syndicate/). Read the original [here](https://jelpblog.wordpress.com/2016/11/24/the-importance-of-gis-in-emergency-management/) and leave a comment.

GIS (geographic information system) is a computer system for “capturing, storing, checking, and displaying data related to positions on the Earth’s surface.”[[1]](#_ftn1)  It allows multiple layers of information to be displayed at once, enabling one to visualize and understand relationships on a map.[[2]](#_ftn2)  Different types of information can be overlaid in the program regardless of their original format or source.[[3]](#_ftn3)  According to ESRI, GIS is described as the “go-to technology” for location-based decisions and is fundamental in understanding the current and future issues involving geographic space.[[4]](#_ftn4)

The modern growth of geospatial technology positively interacts with, and influences all aspects of disaster management – such as mitigation (modeling hazards and vulnerability to develop strategies), preparedness (formulating emergency response and evacuation plans), response (executing such plans), and recovery (assessing damages, rebuilding, preventing recurrence, and educating the public).[[5]](#_ftn5)  Considering we cannot prevent natural disasters, it is important to determine potential hazards and where they stand in relation to our communities.  As a visualization tool, GIS can assist in locating, identifying, and understanding relationships between areas of social vulnerability and potential hazard exposure.  For example, available U.S. census data can be layered onto a map to include the distribution of age, income, ethnicity, housing quality, transportation capacity, etc.[[6]](#_ftn6)  This information can be used to create appropriate mitigation strategies, to identify how or where certain areas should be evacuated, or even how first responders (law enforcement, medical personnel, fire service etc.) should approach certain areas during a disaster.[[7]](#_ftn7)

Furthermore, with advances in GIS and computer technology today, individuals and communities can potentially use the increasingly accessible tools to manage their own knowledge and community data.[[8]](#_ftn8)  If promoted within communities, GIS can be utilized to communicate risks and hazards to the population with no requisite specialized knowledge.[[9]](#_ftn9)  Currently, many communities and homeowners lack the knowledge and motivation to take appropriate cautions or mitigate potential hazards.  Having access to personalized and compelling visuals may ameliorate the issue, while providing local governments invaluable information for disaster management and preparedness.[[10]](#_ftn10)

#### **Challenges and Future Steps – A Look at FEMA Flood Mapping**

First and foremost, data is the most essential element of GIS mapping – the program itself merely creates a visual display of the inputted data.[[11]](#_ftn11)  Without accurate data, the program cannot produce accurate depictions of the desired information or relationships between them.  Therefore, the greatest challenge is the weakness of current data, or the lack of data in general.  Although currently improving, there is also a deficiency of readily available GIS software, and more importantly, a failure in the communication/utilization of GIS and the information it can provide.[[12]](#_ftn12)

Considering the significant role GIS already plays in emergency management, I believe the government, as well as local governments, should be allocating funds to improve each of these three issues.  First, to increase data collection and to improve the accuracy of existing data, second, to promote the use of GIS software by communities, and third, to improve the accessibility and communication of the information produced.  In regards to these aspects, I would like to discuss the ongoing Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) flood mapping as part of the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).

The NFIP was created to provide a means for homeowners to financially protect themselves from flood events – flood insurance is offered to property owners if the community participates in the NFIP and meets floodplain management ordinances established by FEMA.[[13]](#_ftn13)

FEMA’s flood hazard mapping program, Risk Mapping, Assessment and Planning (MAP), identifies flood hazards and assesses risks of certain areas.[[14]](#_ftn14)  This mapping is used to create the Flood Insurance Rate Maps (FIRMs), the basis of NFIP regulations and insurance requirements.[[15]](#_ftn15)  The FIRMs are then used to determine insurance premiums and set minimum floodplain standards for communities based on the assessed risks of the particular location.[[16]](#_ftn16)   Currently, the NFIP states that it is working towards updating the accuracy of flood maps and providing policyholders with information to better understand the program.[[17]](#_ftn17)

1. Improving Accuracy of GIS Data: In cost-benefit analysis, hazard mapping is found to have positive net benefits, thereby indicating that it is beneficial to work towards improving the accuracy of our mapping.[[18]](#_ftn18) A study conducted by FEMA in 2000 found that when considering all costs (flood data updates, map maintenance, new mapping, conversion to new standards, and customer service), the flood maps created a benefit of 1.33 billion dollars, with a cost of 799 million.[[19]](#_ftn19) Currently, flood maps are used an estimated 30 million times a year by government agencies, FEMA contractors, lenders, insurance agents, land developers, community planners, property owners, realtors, and by others for risk assessment, land management, mitigation, and disaster response.[[20]](#_ftn20)  With this in mind, it is clear that the accuracy of these maps is vital and relevant to widespread decisions.

For example, improving the accuracy of FEMA’s flood maps is predicted to directly affect the insurance rates and land use.[[21]](#_ftn21)  More accurate estimates of flood risk allow appropriate insurance premiums to be calculated for certain areas and particular structures.[[22]](#_ftn22)  The accuracy of price may also increase the understanding and trust of flood risk, and therefore encourage and ensure insurance coverage.[[23]](#_ftn23)  In connection to land use, the correctly priced insurance premiums accurately reflect risk, and in turn, reduce the development of land in high-risk areas.[[24]](#_ftn24)  Improvements in accuracy can add restrictions to properties that should have been designated at-risk (reducing future losses of life and property), and conversely, lifting restrictions in areas that were incorrectly designated at-risk (lowering costs and mandatory improvements, enabling the land to be used in other ways).[[25]](#_ftn25)  In fact, FEMA’s website includes an option to contest floodplain boundaries if homeowners believe their properties were incorrectly identified in high-risk areas – increasing accuracy of flood maps may therefore reduce the contesting of boundaries and save time, money, and effort of all parties.

Learning from the NFIP and FEMA’s FIRM flood maps, we can see that it is indeed beneficial to invest in data collection for GIS use in emergency management.  This can be applied to any context, rather than solely floods and national flood insurance – perhaps to fire or earthquake risks, or anything relevant to a community’s planning.

2&3. Promoting Use of GIS Software and Improving Communication of Risks: Little research has been done to show how to effectively communicate risk to the public through hazard maps.[[26]](#_ftn26)  However, previous studies have shown that in particular, there are issues with communicating via FEMA’s FIRM flood maps.[[27]](#_ftn27)  Taking it upon myself to investigate the FEMA website, I found it very difficult to navigate and understand.  There is an overwhelming amount of information and it is unclear how or who it is intended to be used by.  Through the Flood Map Service Center ‘Search by Address’ page, a homeowner can simply type in their address to pull up an interactive flood map, National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL).  This is where the seemingly simple task becomes complicated.  I downloaded the map corresponding to my current apartment address only to find that I had absolutely no idea what I was looking at, or what any of the data meant.  I then managed to locate an FAQ page on the website, linking a 54 page document available for download titled, “How to Read a Flood Insurance Rate Map Tutorial.”  It is quite possible that I did not spend enough time attempting to read and understand the guidance provided by the FEMA webpage, but it is clear why a homeowner or individual with little to no experience in this area would fail to understand the implications of the data.

Furthermore, FEMA’s in-house mapping software, HAZUS, is available to the public for download.  HAZUS, utilizing GIS systems, is described as a “nationally applicable standardized methodology that estimates potential losses from earthquakes, hurricane winds, and floods.”[[28]](#_ftn28)  Looking to download and examine the software, I found that it requires ESRI’s ArcGIS program to run, and that the FEMA site directs users to ESRI where it can be purchased.  From this, I can assume that the HAZUS program is primarily used by and targeted towards local governments, rather than individuals and homeowners.  Although this makes sense, it again limits the accessibility of invaluable information that can be provided – and even local governments may choose not to pursue the costs of analyzing the public data through GIS mapping (costs of the program and of individuals educated to use the program, time to overlay data, etc.).

Again, based solely off of FEMA’s FIRM maps and HAZUS program, we can see that the accessibility of GIS programs, as well as the communication of risk information, is at issue.  As suggested by Susan Cutter, a Geography Professor at the University of South Carolina, emergency managers should look to community partners such as universities to assist with mapping and analysis needs.[[29]](#_ftn29)  From personal experience, she describes the ongoing partnership between the University of South Carolina and the South Carolina Emergency Management Division, allowing the two to work towards a common goal – students can gain experience, while the organization can utilize the resources produced.[[30]](#_ftn30)  She further suggests that if costs of the program or ability to use a program are at issue (such as ESRI ArcGIS), other mapping tools or platforms may be available.[[31]](#_ftn31)  For example, I downloaded QGIS, a free GIS program rather than the common, but pricey ArcGIS.

**Conclusion**

            Starting with FEMA, and moving towards states and local governments, GIS investment should be prioritized for use in disaster management.  Funds ideally should be directed towards increasing data and improving accuracy of that already existing, towards making GIS programs available for use (or finding assistance through partnerships), and towards promoting the communications of risk assessment with the public.

# GIS In Disaster Management Market (By Technology: Geospatial Data, GIS Software, GIS Services, By Application: Emergency Response, Risk Assessment, Resource Management, By End-User: Government, Healthcare, Energy, Environmental Agencies) - Global Market Size, Share, Growth, Trends, Statistics Analysis Report, By Region, And Forecast 2025-2033

## Market Overview

**The global GIS (Geographic Information Systems) in Disaster Management Market was valued at approximately USD 10.2 billion in 2023, with an anticipated market size of USD 23.4 billion by 2033, reflecting a robust compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 8.6% from 2025 to 2033.**

This growth is largely driven by the increasing frequency of natural disasters, climate change effects, and the growing need for effective disaster management solutions. Governments, healthcare agencies, energy sectors, and environmental agencies are adopting GIS solutions to plan and respond to disaster scenarios more effectively. GIS technology offers crucial spatial data and visualizations that assist in risk assessment, resource allocation, and operational coordination during disaster events.

In recent years, GIS has become an integral tool in the disaster management process, providing solutions for risk prediction, real-time monitoring, and resource management. These factors, coupled with technological advancements in cloud computing and IoT (Internet of Things), are expected to further propel the GIS market in disaster management, particularly in emerging economies where disaster preparedness remains a key priority. The market's growth is expected to continue as advancements in GIS technology enable more precise predictions and faster emergency responses.

The application of GIS in disaster management spans multiple sectors, including government, healthcare, energy, and environmental agencies, all of which are expected to boost the market's growth trajectory. As governments worldwide focus on enhancing disaster resilience and response capabilities, the integration of GIS technology will become more prevalent across different stages of disaster management.

## Market Driver

The growth of the GIS in disaster management market is largely driven by several key factors. A major driver is the increasing frequency and intensity of natural disasters, such as hurricanes, earthquakes, and floods, exacerbated by climate change. According to the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR), the world has seen a dramatic rise in the number and severity of natural disasters, with over 50,000 disasters occurring globally in the last two decades. The need for effective disaster management solutions to mitigate loss and improve recovery efforts is more critical than ever.

In addition, governments and disaster relief organizations are increasingly leveraging GIS to improve their preparedness and response strategies. GIS enables organizations to access real-time data, assess risks, and deploy resources quickly in the event of a disaster. This technology allows for more accurate mapping and analysis of affected areas, enabling faster decision-making and enhancing operational efficiency during crises.

Furthermore, GIS's ability to integrate with other technologies such as IoT and machine learning is creating opportunities for smarter, data-driven decision-making, further boosting the adoption of GIS in disaster management.

## Market Restraint

Despite its vast potential, the GIS in disaster management market faces certain challenges that could hinder its growth. One of the primary restraints is the high initial cost of GIS technology implementation, which includes software licensing, hardware setup, and skilled labor requirements. Many organizations, especially in developing countries, face budget constraints that limit their ability to adopt advanced GIS solutions. This is particularly relevant for smaller local governments or humanitarian organizations that might struggle to justify the initial investment in GIS infrastructure.

Additionally, the complexity of GIS systems can be a significant barrier for organizations lacking technical expertise. Effective implementation of GIS in disaster management requires specialized training, data management skills, and knowledge of GIS tools, which may not always be readily available.

Another challenge is data privacy and security concerns related to GIS platforms. As GIS systems involve the collection and analysis of large amounts of sensitive data, ensuring the security of this information is a critical issue. Cybersecurity threats, such as hacking and data breaches, pose a risk to both the integrity of GIS systems and the privacy of individuals.

## Market Opportunity

The GIS in disaster management market presents significant opportunities for growth, especially as technology continues to advance. One of the major opportunities is the growing demand for real-time data and predictive analytics in disaster management. GIS can provide highly accurate, real-time mapping and analysis that can improve disaster preparedness, response, and recovery. As such, the market for GIS solutions in disaster management is expanding rapidly, particularly in regions vulnerable to frequent natural disasters, such as Southeast Asia and the Pacific Islands.

Furthermore, the integration of GIS with other technologies such as remote sensing, big data analytics, and artificial intelligence (AI) offers new avenues for market growth. These technologies allow for more precise disaster modeling, better resource allocation, and faster response times during crises. Governments and organizations are increasingly focused on using data-driven approaches to mitigate disaster risks, which enhances the overall appeal of GIS in this sector.

The expansion of cloud-based GIS solutions also represents a significant market opportunity. Cloud computing offers scalability, flexibility, and cost-efficiency, making it easier for governments and organizations to adopt GIS technology without the need for substantial upfront investments in infrastructure.

## Market Segment Analysis

**Geospatial Data**

Geospatial data plays a pivotal role in GIS-based disaster management systems. Geospatial data refers to information that is linked to a specific location on the Earth's surface. This includes topography, weather patterns, population density, infrastructure data, and more. Accurate geospatial data enables organizations to assess disaster risks, monitor events in real-time, and predict the potential impact of a disaster. It also helps in planning evacuation routes, identifying safe zones, and managing post-disaster recovery efforts.

The importance of geospatial data in disaster management is increasingly recognized, leading to growing investments in data collection and management technologies. Remote sensing technologies, such as satellites and drones, are key sources of geospatial data, offering real-time updates and high-resolution imagery of affected regions. The demand for these data sources is expected to drive growth in the GIS market for disaster management applications.

**GIS Software**

GIS software is another key segment driving the growth of the GIS in disaster management market. GIS software enables the collection, processing, and analysis of geospatial data to make informed decisions during disaster scenarios. These software platforms provide various functionalities, such as spatial data analysis, mapping, and visualization, which are essential for effective disaster management.

Advancements in GIS software have made it more user-friendly, accessible, and capable of handling large datasets in real-time. Cloud-based GIS software has further contributed to the market’s growth by enabling users to access and collaborate on disaster management projects remotely. The ongoing development of specialized disaster management software, tailored to specific types of disasters (e.g., hurricanes, floods, wildfires), will continue to foster demand in this segment.

## Regional Analysis

The global GIS in disaster management market shows varying trends across regions, with North America dominating the market in terms of both size and growth. This region's robust infrastructure, technological advancements, and high investment in disaster management initiatives make it a key player. The U.S. government, for instance, has heavily invested in GIS technologies for disaster preparedness and emergency response. According to the U.S. Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), GIS technology is critical in response planning, resource allocation, and recovery efforts during natural disasters.

In Europe, the market is driven by an emphasis on environmental sustainability, climate change mitigation, and the increasing frequency of floods and wildfires. Countries like Germany and the U.K. are actively adopting GIS solutions to improve disaster resilience and response strategies.

In Asia Pacific, the demand for GIS technology is increasing rapidly, driven by frequent natural disasters such as earthquakes, tsunamis, and floods. Countries like Japan, India, and the Philippines are leveraging GIS to improve disaster preparedness and response times. The Asia Pacific region is expected to witness significant growth during the forecast period.

Latin America and the Middle East & Africa regions are still in the early stages of GIS adoption for disaster management. However, with increasing natural disaster occurrences, these regions are expected to adopt GIS solutions more widely, offering significant opportunities for growth in the coming years.

To maintain a competitive edge, companies are investing in research and development, strategic partnerships, and acquisitions. Many companies are also focusing on expanding their presence in emerging markets, particularly in regions prone to frequent natural disasters.

## Future Outlook

The integration of GIS with AI, machine learning, and big data analytics is expected to revolutionize the disaster management process, enabling quicker, more accurate responses. Additionally, the increasing frequency of natural disasters, coupled with global climate change, will further drive the demand for GIS solutions to mitigate risks and manage resources effectively.

Governments, humanitarian organizations, and private enterprises will continue to focus on improving disaster resilience through the adoption of GIS technology. As these technologies evolve, the market for GIS in disaster management will expand, with more innovative solutions emerging to address the complexities of modern disaster management.

Using GIS expedites response and recovery efforts, monitors the status of community lifelines, and helps you communicate recovery resources to the public. ## GIS for Emergency Management ebook ## Emergency and Disaster Management solutions Temporarily extend existing licenses or gain access to ArcGIS, the world's leading GIS technology, to support GIS requirements during a disaster response. FULL CONTENT:
![](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/industry-emergency-management-overview-banner-medium-background.jpg)

# Emergency and Disaster Management

Build resilience, respond, and recover with real-time GIS technology

![A map with highlighted hotspots, sandbags holding bag water, and a dispatcher holding a phone and a radio](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/emergency-management-overview-foreground.png)

Focusing efforts to mitigate the impacts and protect against the complex threats and hazards in today's modern world requires a deeper understanding of the social, economic, and environmental systems around us. Emergency managers use geographic information system (GIS) technology with data-driven insights to better prepare and help their communities respond and recover from significant events.

### Build resilience with data-driven insight

Understand, identify, and build strategies that mitigate natural hazards and human-caused threats using the science of geography. Using GIS, areas of risk and vulnerability are highlighted by combining critical infrastructure, hazard, and demographics data into a single map.

### Respond with real-time information

Anticipate needs during rapidly changing conditions with real-time dashboards that aid critical decision-making and maximize the effectiveness of emergency management operations. GIS builds shared situational awareness across response teams, stakeholders, and the public.

### Reestablish lifelines with mobile tools

Conduct damage assessments, build a reporting database, and monitor debris removal with field data collecting and reporting applications. Using GIS expedites response and recovery efforts, monitors the status of community lifelines, and helps you communicate recovery resources to the public.

## US Coast Guard drives critical bridge response with GIS

USCG's geospatial data-driven response reopened Baltimore's port in 76 days, showcasing interagency collaboration and technology use.

![Panorama view of a bustling modern riverside city beginning to light up for nighttime under a soft orange and blue sunset sky](/content/dam/esrisites/en-us/industries/2022/public-safety/emergency-management/assets/overview/uscg-key-bridge-response-50-50-split.jpg)

## GIS for Emergency Management ebook

This ebook describes how to leverage data and location intelligence for analysis, improve emergency response and recovery outcomes, and effectively utilize data to build long-term resilience.

## Esri Disaster Response Program (DRP)

Esri was founded to help solve some of the world's most difficult problems. For over 25 years, the Esri DRP has provided GIS support to our users and the global community during disasters and crises of all types and sizes.

### ArcGIS software

Temporarily extend existing licenses or gain access to ArcGIS, the world's leading GIS technology, to support GIS requirements during a disaster response.

### Workflow implementation

Discover modern capabilities to support disaster response. We can implement or help you configure battle-tested solutions during your response.

### Geospatial data

Respond with incident-specific data—such as real-time weather, traffic, hazards, infrastructure information, and demographics—from the response community and private sector.

### Technical support

Gain access to our industry-leading premium support services. A dedicated team of professionals is ready to address any question or issue related to ArcGIS technology.

Collection 

# Real-time GIS for disaster management

Submission status
:   Open

Submission deadline

**This Collection supports and amplifies research related to  
[SDG 9 - Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure](https://sdgs.un.org/goals/goal9) & [SDG 13 - Climate Action](https://sdgs.un.org/goals/goal13)**



Real-time Geographic Information Systems (GIS) plays a crucial role in disaster prevention, preparedness, response, and recovery. It leverages data from diverse sources such as satellites, drones, sensors, and social media to provide up-to-the-minute information on disaster events, enabling authorities to make informed decisions swiftly during emergencies. The processing of these large datasets, however, is still challenging and requires more robust technological infrastructure that might not be easily accessible. As technology advances, the integration of GIS with other emerging technologies, such as artificial intelligence and the Internet of Things, will further enhance its capabilities, making communities more resilient to future disasters.

This Collection invites original research on the application of real-time GIS in disaster prevention, management, and recovery, including, but not limited to, extreme event monitoring, evacuation planning, resource allocation, and hazard mapping. We particularly encourage studies that introduce innovative methods and models, especially those validated by real-world scenario datasets, as these can provide invaluable insights for enhancing disaster response strategies.

 —

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://www.topbimcompany.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Disaster-management-cycle-with-GIS.png
DESCRIPTION: The graphic illustrates the disaster management cycle using GIS, featuring key elements such as predictive preparedness, smart mitigation, swift recovery, and rapid response, all interconnected in a dynamic flow around a colorful representation of geospatial data.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://www.igismap.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/03/Disaster-manage.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The graphic illustrates the concept of GIS in disaster management, featuring a stylized urban landscape alongside detailed maps showing the impact of the 2006 floods in the Horn of Africa, including travel times to key cities and the extent of flooding.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.sgligis.com/wp-content/uploads/Featured-Image.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The collage highlights various aspects of disaster management and emergency response, showcasing flooding, damaged infrastructure, healthcare workers in protective gear, disease mapping, and the power of GIS technology in addressing natural disasters like hurricanes.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.sgligis.com/wp-content/uploads/10-e1650430733580.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram outlines the roles and functions of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) in disaster management, highlighting key areas such as types of disasters, communication links, databases, and strategies for ground truth verification, all contributing to effective disaster response and recovery.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Abhinav-Walia/publication/328161813/figure/fig1/AS:679697860014084@1539064006887/Role-of-GIS-in-each-phase-of-Disaster-Emergency-Management_Q320.jpg

enriched_points.expanded_content: Allocate funding for GIS tools and infrastructure to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities, ensuring accurate data collection and analysis during emergencies.
Implement comprehensive training programs for emergency managers and community members, ensuring proficiency in GIS software and effective utilization of spatial data in disaster scenarios.
Foster local engagement by involving residents in disaster planning and response efforts, leveraging their knowledge to enhance GIS data accuracy and community resilience.
Promote inter-agency partnerships to share GIS resources and data, improving coordination and efficiency in disaster response and recovery operations across multiple sectors.
Prioritize long-term resilience strategies by investing in adaptive infrastructure and community education, making effective use of GIS for ongoing risk assessment and mitigation.
